summary: The US trade office is pushing tighter US-Mexico-Canada Agreement rules on auto content, citing falling US-made shares in vehicles built in Mexico and Canada. President Sheinbaum says talks with US trade representative Jamieson Greer are advancing in the pact's first annual review, even as Washington prepares a tariff of up to 10% over forced-labor concerns on coffee and tomatoes. Separately, US and Mexican treasuries sanctioned more than 50 people and companies linked to the Jalisco New Generation Cartel's financial network, including the stepson of leader

lead.h1: US trade office recommends tighter North American auto content rules
lead.context: The trade office cites a shrinking US content share in vehicles assembled in Mexico and Canada and regional reliance on chips and circuit boards sourced outside North America.
lead.background: The US-Mexico-Canada Agreement sets how much regional content a vehicle needs to qualify for tariff-free treatment, and the pact now undergoes annual reviews after Washington declined a 16-year extension.
lead.drivers: US trade office points to a shrinking US content share in vehicles built in Mexico and Canada and to regional reliance on chips, circuit boards and display panels from non-market economies.
lead.implications: Mexico faces 25% duties on vehicles and parts that fail to meet the qualifying content threshold.

coverage.title: USTR recommends tighter USMCA auto rules of origin
coverage.summary: The trade office cites a shrinking US content share in vehicles assembled in Mexico and Canada and regional reliance on chips and circuit boards sourced outside North America.

items.headline: Sheinbaum says Mexico and US advance in talks to review the US-Mexico-Canada trade agreement
items.context: The meeting with US trade representative Jamieson Greer is part of the trade pact's first annual review, coming after Trump declined to extend the agreement for another 16 years.
items.drivers: The meeting, lasting just over an hour, brought together Ebrard, Velasco and other Mexican officials with Greer and US Ambassador Ronald Johnson to discuss the pact's bilateral review and related agreements.

coverage.title: Sheinbaum says Mexico and US advance in USMCA review talks
coverage.summary: The meeting with US trade representative Jamieson Greer is part of the trade pact's first annual review, coming after Trump declined to extend the agreement for another 16 years.

items.headline: US prepares new tariff of up to 10% over forced-labor concerns on coffee and tomatoes
items.context: Washington maintains a block on Mexican companies over forced-labor allegations and is preparing an additional tariff, adding pressure during the review of the US-Mexico-Canada Agreement.
items.drivers: A US trade office report says Mexico has not effectively enforced its ban on forced-labor goods, allowing them to compete unfairly and artificially lower costs including labor costs.
items.implications: US Customs and Border Protection maintains two Withhold Release Orders against Mexican companies while Washington prepares tariffs of up to 10% on exports that fail to meet US-Mexico-Canada trade agreement labor provisions.

coverage.title: US prepares new tariff of up to 10% over forced-labor concerns on coffee and tomatoes
coverage.summary: Washington maintains a block on Mexican companies over forced-labor allegations and is preparing an additional tariff, adding pressure during the USMCA review.

items.headline: Forced-labor complaint over Chinese camera imports tests labor rules in the US-Mexico-Canada trade agreement
items.context: Civil groups file what they call the first citizen-driven case under the trade pact, accusing Mexico of allowing imports linked to forced labor, a claim that could trigger formal review procedures with the US.
items.background: Article 23.6 of the US-Mexico-Canada Agreement lets citizens file complaints over forced-labor imports, and the US has proposed a 10% minimum tariff for most partners on goods linked to forced labor.
items.drivers: Civil groups ProDESC and Empower revived a complaint over Dahua and Hikvision surveillance cameras tied to Xinjiang supply chains, filed with the labor ministry in 2023 and dismissed that same year.
items.implications: The case adds to US scrutiny of Mexico during the review of the US-Mexico-Canada Agreement at the same time Mexico is trying to avoid the proposed 10% forced-labor tariff.

coverage.title: Forced-labor complaint over Chinese camera imports tests USMCA mechanism
coverage.summary: Civil groups file what they call the first citizen-driven case under the trade pact, accusing Mexico of allowing imports linked to forced labor, a claim that could trigger formal review procedures with the US.

items.headline: US and Mexican treasuries sanction Jalisco cartel's financial network
items.context: The action targets more than 50 people and companies tied to the Jalisco New Generation Cartel, including the stepson of its leader known as 'El Mencho,' marking one of the largest joint financial strikes against the group.
items.background: The Jalisco New Generation Cartel (CJNG) is one of Mexico's largest criminal organizations, led by Nemesio Oseguera Cervantes, known as 'El Mencho'.
items.drivers: Mexico's Financial Intelligence Unit filed a complaint with the federal attorney general's office alleging money laundering tied to the sanctioned network, while the US Treasury froze the assets of those listed.
items.implications: The sanctions cut off a network of individuals and companies accused of financing the cartel through money laundering.
items.next: Mexico's Attorney General's office will pursue prosecution of the sanctioned individuals following the Financial Intelligence Unit's complaint.

coverage.title: US and Mexican treasuries sanction Jalisco cartel's financial network
coverage.summary: The action targets more than 50 people and companies tied to the Jalisco New Generation Cartel, including the stepson of its leader known as 'El Mencho,' marking one of the largest joint financial strikes against the group.
